paper_title: Long Live The Balance: Information Bottleneck Driven Tree-based Policy Optimization

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent advances in online reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated promising performance in complex reasoning tasks. However, they often exhibit an imbalanced exploration-exploitation trade-off, resulting in unstable optimization and sub-optimal performance. We introduce IB-Score, a novel metric grounded in Information Bottleneck theory that evaluates policy's exploration-exploitation balance by quantifying the trade-off between step-level reasoning diversity and mutual information shared with the correct answer. Analysis based on IB-Score shows that popular online RL approaches (e.g., GRPO) with common regularizers fail to consistently maintain balance during training with suboptimal results. To address this, we propose Information Bottleneck-driven Tree-based Policy Optimization (IB-TPO), a principled framework that formulates IB-Score as a fine-grained optimization objective and utilizes a novel IB-guided tree sampling strategy that not only improves the efficiency of online sampling with 50% more trajectories under the same token budget, but also reuses the tree structure for effective IB-Score Monte Carlo estimation. Extensive experiments across standard benchmarks show that our method significantly outperforms GRPO baseline by 2.9% to 3.6% and also outperforms other state-of-the-art online RL approaches. Our code is available at https://github.com/alibaba/EfficientRL.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent advances in online reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated promising performance in complex reasoning tasks. However, they often exhibit an imbalanced explorationexploitation trade-off, resulting in unstable optimization and sub-optimal performance. We introduce IB-Score, novel metric grounded in Information Bottleneck theory that evaluates policys exploration-exploitation balance by quantifying the trade-off between steplevel reasoning diversity and mutual information shared with the correct answer. Analysis based on IB-Score shows that popular online RL approaches (e.g., GRPO) with common regularizers fail to consistently maintain balance during training with suboptimal results. To address this, we propose Information Bottleneck-driven Treebased Policy Optimization (IB-TPO), principled framework that formulates IB-Score as fine-grained optimization objective and utilizes novel IB-guided tree sampling strategy that not only improves the efficiency of online sampling with 50% more trajectories under the same token budget, but also reuses the tree structure for effective IB-Score Monte Carlo estimation. Extensive experiments across standard benchmarks show that our method significantly outperforms GRPO baseline by 2.9% to 3.6% and also outperforms other state-of-the-art online RL approaches. Our code is available at Github. 1. Introduction Recent state-of-the-art approaches in online reinforcement learning (RL) have propelled large language models (LLMs) 1Alibaba Cloud Computing, Alibaba Group. Correspondence to: Minying Zhang <minying.zmy@alibaba-inc.com>. Proceedings of the 43 rd International Conference on Machine Learning, Seoul, South Korea. PMLR 306, 2026. Copyright 2026 by the author(s). 1 Figure 1. Striking an effective balance between exploration and exploitation remains key challenge in online RL. We propose IB-Score to evaluate and encourage balanced policy optimization. to achieve breakthrough performance in complex reasoning tasks such as mathematical reasoning (Luo et al., 2023; Shao et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024), instruction following (Ren et al., 2025), function calling (Qian et al., 2025) and multimodal reasoning (Wei et al., 2025). Among them, Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) (Shao et al., 2024) has emerged leading approach but leaves fundamental challenges: (1) Inadequate exploration, as independent sampling strategy struggles to produce diverse trajectories with sufficient exploration due to structural inefficiency (Hou et al., 2025) and rapid model convergence (Cui et al., 2025; Cheng et al., 2025). (2) Insufficient exploitation, as sparse rewards from outcome-based verifiers limit fine-grained policy optimization (Li et al., 2025). Existing approaches attempt to overcome these issues from different perspectives, e.g., introducing entropy-based mechanisms to boost exploration (Cui et al., 2025; Cheng et al., 2025), employing process reward models to identify erroneous or critical reasoning steps (Shao et al., 2025), or incorporating tree structures to enable fine-grained reward estimation while strengthening exploration (Hou et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025). However, they still suffer from either the attenuation of learning signals due to policys early convergence to high-certainty local optimum (Yu et al., 2025; Xi et al., 2025) or training instability with degraded performance caused by exploding uncertainty (Zheng et al., 2025) Long Live The Balance: Information Bottleneck Driven Tree-based Policy Optimization (Figure 1). Consequently, there is an urgent need for unified mechanism to quantify and encourage balanced trade-off between exploration and exploitation (Deng et al., 2026; Lei et al., 2025). To address this, we diagnose the imbalanced online RL of LLMs from the perspective of Information Bottleneck (IB) theory (Tishby et al., 2000) in Section 3. We propose IBScore, novel metric that effectively quantifies the step-wise balance between policy exploration and exploitation. Specifically, IB-Score measures both diversity of policys finegrained exploration and the mutual information between each reasoning step and the correct answer. To investigate the relationship between IB-Score and model convergence, we conducted experiments to analyze the training dynamics of online RL baselines. Using IB-Score, we explain the underlying causes behind models over-exploration and over-exploitation. Evidence suggests that maintaining relatively stable IB-Score is beneficial for achieving sustained exploration diversity and higher performance. Based on these, we propose Information Bottleneck driven Tree-based Policy Optimization (IB-TPO) in Section 4, which incorporates IB-Score into the RL objective to encourage the policy to adaptively strike balance between exploration and exploitation throughout reasoning process. To this end, IB-TPO employs an IB-guided tree-based sampling strategy (IBTree) which not only selectively explores the models most diverse and promising reasoning directions under the guidance of IB-Score, but also serves as an efficient Monte Carlo estimator of IB-Score. This mutual reinforcement between IB-Score estimation and tree-based sampling boosts exploration effectiveness of RL training. We train our models at various scales of Qwen3 (Yang et al., 2025a) models and conduct comprehensive evaluations on multiple in/out-of-domain benchmarks. Experiments demonstrate that IB-TPO exhibits clear advantages by 2.9-3.6% over the GRPO baselines as well as state-ofthe-art online RL methods. Moreover, our method not only achieves higher sampling effectiveness, but also maintains better explorationexploitation trade-off. This highlights the superiority of integrating IB with tree-based online RL. In summary, our main contributions are threefold: We propose novel metric IB-Score to quantify and diagnose the imbalanced exploration-exploitation trade-off in fine-grained level during LLM online RL. We propose IB-TPO, novel tree-based policy optimization framework that incorporates IB-Score into the RL objective. IB-TPO leverages IBTree that enables IB-guided exploration and efficient IB-Score estimation. We conduct comprehensive experiments to demonstrate the superiority of our method over GRPO baselines and state-of-the-art approaches. Conflict of Interest Disclosure. All authors of this paper are employed by Alibaba Cloud Computing, which leads the development of the Qwen3 series of models. The Qwen3-1.7B-Base and Qwen3-8B-Base models, as well as the Qwen3-14B-Base model used in the extended experiments, were among the models evaluated in this paper. 2. Given problem and the policy model πθ parameterized by θ, GRPO independently samples trajectories {τi}G i=1 from πθ(q) to explore different ways of solving problem. Then, reward signals {Ri}G i=1 are collected from verifier (e.g., outcome math verifier, code executor) and normalized in group to calculate relative advantages compared to group baseline. Finally, the policy optimization with clipping strategy is performed as follows: JGRPO(θ) = Eτ πθ(q) 1 G (cid:88) i=1 1 τi τi (cid:88) t=1 (cid:20) min (wi,t(θ)Ai,t, clip(wi,t(θ), 1 ϵ, 1 + ϵ)Ai,t) (1) βKLDKL(πθπref) (cid:21) , where wi,t(θ) = πθ(τi,tq, τi,<t) πref(τi,tq, τi,<t) , Ai,t = Ri mean({Ri}G std({Ri}G i=1) i=1) , (2) denote the importance weight and advantage of each trajectory token τi,t, respectively. Information Bottleneck (IB) Theory (Tishby & Zaslavsky, 2015; Tishby et al., 2000) provides theoretical framework for extracting relevant information from an input variable to predict an output variable through an intermediate representation Z. The objective is formulated as: min I(X; Z) βI(Z; ), (3) where I( ; ) denotes mutual information. Specifically, minimizing I(X; Z) encourages model generalization by discarding task-irrelevant information, while maximizing I(Z; ) promotes the relevance between the intermediate representation and the target output. This dual objective offers unified perspective for balancing the tradeoff between exploration and exploitation of the model. In practice, to make this objective tractable, the mutual information terms are often expanded using entropy H(): I(X; ) = H(X) H(XY ), serving as the mathematical foundation for IB objective. 2 Long Live The Balance: Information Bottleneck Driven Tree-based Policy Optimization Figure 2. Both clip-higher and entropy regularization fail to improve performance. 3. Diagnosing Exploration-Exploitation Imbalance: An IB Perspective 3.1. Exploration-Exploitation Dilemma in Online RL To uncover the challenges in exploration-exploitation balancing of online RL, we employ Qwen3-8B-Base and conduct experiments with GRPO baseline and its variants with two commonly used regularization methods: clip-higher and entropy regularization. As shown by the training dynamics in Figure 2, the GRPO baseline suffers from performance stagnation. This issue stems from premature convergence in the early stages of training, where rapid decrease in policy entropy causes the model to become overly deterministic (Cui et al., 2025). This leads to to sharp decline in the proportion of effective sampled groups with non-zero reward variance (termed Eff-Rate) and results in sparse learning signal for policy optimization and plateaued validation accuracy. Although techniques like asymmetric clipping thresholds and entropy regularization can alleviate entropy degradation, they fail to improve model performance. Despite the increased entropy, their Eff-Rate continues to decline throughout training, indicating limited exploration effectiveness. Moreover, naively encouraging higher entropy often leads to entropy explosion (where the model exhibits excessive uncertainty) and results in training instability. This highlights fundamental dilemma in current online RL approaches: they not only struggle to incentivize effective exploration, but also lack robust mechanism to balance exploration and exploitation. To address this challenge, we turn to the Information Bottleneck (IB) theory by proposing it as new lens to quantify and navigate this trade-off. 3.2. Quantifying ExplorationExploitation via IB Although existing approaches (Deng et al., 2026; Lei et al., 2025) have demonstrated the superiority of the applying IB theory to LLM reasoning and reinforcement training, they still lack an effective and efficient way for leveraging IB to quantify and encourage the fine-grained explorationexploitation balance in online RL. In this section, we aim to reformulate IB as an interpretable quantitative metric to evaluate this trade-off in fine-grained manner. Given policy model πθ, problem q, multi-step reasoning process τ = {si}Nτ i=1 πθ(q) and the ground-truth answer a, we reformulate the objective of the information bottleneck from Equation 3 as maximizing: JIB(τ ) = I(q; τ ) + βI(τ ; aq) = [H(τ ) H(τ q)] + β[H(τ q) H(τ a, q)] = H(τ ) + (β + 1)H(τ q) βH(τ a, q). The interplay of these two terms allows JIB(si) to function as comprehensive metric, and we term it as the Information Bottleneck Score (IB-Score). Further analysis in Appendix reveals that the IB-Score offers more nuanced objective than simply measuring the models uncertainty (i.e., entropy), as it critically dependent on the covariance Cov(η1, η2). This implies that achieving an effective exploration-exploitation balance requires more than just encouraging high overall uncertainty. Instead, the model must learn to strategically allocate its confidence to branches where the feedback is most informative, thus creating positive correlation between η1 and η2. 3.3. sections.title: Policy Optimization
sections.content: In Section 3, we propose the IB-Score to evaluate online RL training and demonstrate the existing explorationexploitation balancing issue in current online RL approaches. To address this issue, we incorporate the IB-Score into the optimization objective of online RL. However, this is nontrivial, as estimating IB-Score on every step will significantly increase the burden of online sampling and reduce training efficiency. To overcome this, we introduce an efficient tree sampling structure in Section 4.1 that enhances sampling efficiency and enables effective estimation of the IB-Score. And then perform IB-guided online RL with structured sampled trajectories in Section 4.2. 4.1. Information Bottleneck Guided Tree Search Although previous approaches (Li et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2025b) introduce tree structured sampling into online RL of LLMs, they suffer from enormous search space and degraded efficiency. TreeRL (Hou et al., 2025) introduces an entropy-guided tree search to improve exploration efficiency by selectively branching on tokens where the model exhibits the highest uncertainty. However, this strategy ignores environmental feedback and there is misalignment between Algorithm 1 IBTPO Input: Problem q, Policy πθ, Ref Policy πref, Branching Iterations L, Branching Nodes K, Branches Output: Optimized Policy π θ // IBTree Initialization 1: {}, {q} // IBTree Expansion 2: for = 1 to do 3: arg topKsS (IB-Score(s)) (cid:83)S ; nodes(T ) b=1{τbτb πθ(q), pre(S (cid:83)B k= k) τb} 4: 5: 6: end for // Step-level Advantage Calculation 7: for each step in the tree nodes do 8: 9: 10: 11: end for Calculate local advantage AIB(s) by Equation 14 Calculate global advantage AGL(s) by Equation 15 A(s) AIB(s) + λ AGL(s) // Policy Optimization θ Update πθ by Equation 1 with advantage A(s) 12: π token-level entropy and the models decision-making. This IB-guided branching strategy enables tree search to achieve better exploration efficiency. To encourage balanced exploration-exploitation trade-off, we incorporate IB-Score of reasoning steps into optimization objective. Our experiments are mainly conducted on Qwen3-1.7B-Base and Qwen3-8B-Base (Yang et al., 2025a), popular model family that widely employed in RL researches and cover different parameter scales. We select DAPO-Math-17K dataset (Yu et al., 2025) as our training dataset, which contains about 17K challenging mathematical problems paired with ground-truth answers. Training Details. We implemented our training framework on ms-swift (Zhao et al., 2025a) which employs vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023) with prefix caching technology for inference acceleration. During the sampling stage, we sample trajectories with recommended parameters = 0.7, top p=0.95, top k=20 and maximum truncation length of 2K per trajectory. For each training iteration, we sample 128 problems for generating trajectories. For independent sampling approaches, we sample 8 trajectories for each problem, and for tree-based approaches we sample 12 trajectories for each problem with (B0, L, K, B) = (4, 9, 1, 1) which have equivalent token consumption to that of 8 independent trajectories (see Table 3). We set the learning rate to 1 106, the KL regularization weight to 0.001, and we train the model for 1 epoch. All of our experiments are conducted on 8A100 GPUs. Please see Appendix B.1 for more implementation details for our method. Benchmark Evaluation. We employ EvalScope (Team, 2024) to evaluate the performance of our LLMs. We evaluate models on several widely used challenging mathematical benchmarks: MATH-500 (Lightman et al., 2023), AIME 24/25, AMC 23/24 and two out-of-domain benchmarks: GPQA Diamond (Rein et al., 2024) and IFEval (Zhou et al., 2023). We employ standard offline pipeline to evaluate and compare explorationexploitation balance across different models through IB-Score. Specifically, we sample 4 seed trajectories for each problem in random subset from DAPO-Math-17K with 1024 problems, perform 5 rollouts from every non-terminal steps to calculate IB-Scores, and finally average across all steps. 5.2. Main Results Baselines. We compare our method with GRPO baseline as well as various representative state-of-the-art approaches. Specifically, in addition to the GRPO baseline and its variants incorporating clip-higher and entropy regularization, we also compare with IBRO (Lei et al., 2025), TreeRL (Hou et al., 2025) and TreePO (Li et al., 2025). IBRO transforms IB objective as sequence-level advantage-weighted entropy regularization term, TreeRL and TreePO introduce efficient tree search into online RL. To ensure fair comparison, we evaluate them using officially provided opensource codes with recommended parameters, and align the basic experimental settings. We dont include concurrent work IIB-LPO (Deng et al., 2026) in our comparison, as it proposes regularization term similar to IBRO and has not yet released code. Please see Appendix B.2 for more implementation details of compared approaches. Main Comparison. As shown in Table 1, our method significantly outperforms the GRPO baseline, achieving an average improvement of 2.9% and 3.6%, and also surpasses all compared methods across different benchmarks. We Table 2. Ablation of main components of our method. Additionally, our tree sampling method significantly outperforms independent sampling in terms of Eff-Rate (by average of 6.8%), demonstrating the superior exploration diversity of IBTree. Moreover, benefiting from the fine-grained IB objective, our method shows significantly better dynamics on Cov(η1, η2) and IBScore compared to other approaches. This indicates that our method not only achieves higher exploration diversity but also maintains effective confidence allocation capability. We also report pass@K accuracy in Appendix C.2. 5.3. More Analysis Effectiveness of main components. Our method consists of two main components: IBTree and IB-based advantage estimation. We conduct ablation study in Table 2, where we use GRPOs independent sampling and the advantage 7 Long Live The Balance: Information Bottleneck Driven Tree-based Policy Optimization Figure 5. Training dynamics of our method compared with state-of-the-art approaches using Qwen3-8B-base. Table 3. Ablation of different tree branching strategies. Table 4. The results in the table reveal the following observations: (1) Both components improve GRPOs performance. (2) EPTree & IBTPO Adv outperforms TreeRL, demonstrating that the IB-based objective yields non-negligible improvement. (3) The full combination (IBTree & IBTPO Adv) achieves the best results, confirming that IBTree guided by IB-Score cannot be readily replaced by alternative tree structures and further highlighting the synergistic nature of our framework. Effectiveness of IBTree. To demonstrate the effectiveness of IBTree, we compare it with several different sampling strategies in Table 3: (1) Independent sampling with no branching, (2) random branching, (3) branching at every step until the tree width reaches the target fix-width (Li et al., 2025), (4) entropy guided (Hou et al., 2025) branching with most uncertain token, and (5) IB-Score guided branching (our strategy). We use Qwen3-8B-Base on random sampled subset of DAPO-Math-17K dataset with 1024 problems for analysis. More parameters are compared in Appendix C.1. Although random and fix-width save token cost, they suffer from reduced Eff-Rate since shared reasoning prefixes naturally reduces the diversity between trajectories. The entropy guided branching strategy improves diversity and Eff-Rate by selectively branching at high8 0 (only global advantage) 0.05 0.1 0.5 15.0% 14.9% 15.3% 13.7% 40.8% 43.8% 41.9% 45.6% 46.0% 41.7% 39.8% 42.0% Table 5. Ablation of step delimiter. Models AIME 25 AMC 24 GPQA w/o random noise w/ random noise 15.3% 15.5% 46.0% 45.7% 41.7% 41.5% entropy tokens. Our IB-Score guided leverages IBScore as branching guidance, achieving the best Eff-Rate and improving the overall accuracy (Avg-Rate). Moreover, under similar token budget as independent sampling, our tree sampling strategy generates 50% more trajectories. We also compare GPU wall-clock runtime in Appendix C.3. Notably, we introduce implementation optimizations to improve the efficiency of IBTree, enabling it to achieve better performance while attaining higher runtime efficiency than independent sampling. Ablation of parameter β. The parameter β controls the weights of two terms in Equation 5. We compare different β in Table 3, the results show that β = 5 produces better Eff-Rate and Avg-Rate with less token consumption. Ablation of parameter λ. We conduct an ablation study of advantage weight λ in Equation 16. As shown in Table 4, training with only global advantage achieves sub-optimal results and λ = 0.1 achieves the best performance. Ablation of step delimiter. We use nn as step delimiter in our framework, which is simple, training-free, and natural formatting boundary in chain-of-thought responses. To validate its robustness, we additionally conduct noiseLong Live The Balance: Information Bottleneck Driven Tree-based Policy Optimization injection experiment in Table 5, where we perturb 10% of existing nn split points by relocating them to random positions to simulate under-segmentation and oversegmentation noise. As shown, the performance change is not significant, indicating that our method is robust to moderate step-boundary perturbations. 6. Related Work 6.1. Online RL for LLM Reasoning Previous advances (Guo et al., 2025a; Shao et al., 2024; Schulman et al., 2017) have demonstrated the superiority of online reinforcement learning (RL) in large language models (LLMs) post-training. Although promising, recent approaches (Yue et al., 2025; Deng et al., 2025; Yu et al., 2025) indicate that balanced exploration-exploitation benefits RL training. To this end, recent work (Deng et al., 2026; Lei et al., 2025) propose advantage-weighted entropy regularization terms based on the IB to encourage this balance. Process supervision are also proved to be an effective way to enhance LLM reasoning (Lightman et al., 2023) and online RL (Zhang et al., 2025; Park et al., 2025), but it remains key challenge to efficiently collect process supervision. 6.2. Tree Search in Online RL Recent works (Qi et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2024; Hao et al., 2023) have demonstrated that tree search enhances the reasoning capabilities of LLMs, as it not only extends the depth of reasoning through structured exploration but also leverages tree structures to provide process supervision. (Yang et al., 2025b; Ji et al., 2025; Guo et al., 2025b) further integrate tree search into online RL. However, most of them rely on large tree search space, hindering this integration. To address this, TreeRL (Hou et al., 2025) introduces an entropy-guided branching strategy to boost exploration of tree searching in limited token budget, and TreePO (Li et al., 2025) controls sampling cost with limited tree width. 7. Conclusion This work addresses imbalanced explorationthe exploitation in online RL. We introduce fine-grained metric IB-Score to evaluate this trade-off and propose IB-TPO, unified framework integrating IB-Score into policy optimization with IBTree. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of IB-TPO, highlighting the promise of integrating IB with tree-based online RL. 8. Limitation Despite effective, we still need to address the time consumption introduced by multi-iteration tree sampling. Although parallel sampling multiple trees can fully utilize decoding width of inference engine, IBTree remains slightly slower than independent sampling under same token budget. Moreover, we will extend IB-TPO to more scenarios in future, such as multimodal reasoning and function calling.

sections.title: Impact Statement
sections.content: This paper advances online reinforcement learning for large language models by introducing IB-Score, an information-bottleneck-based metric for measuring the explorationexploitation balance, and IB-TPO, tree-based policy optimization framework that leverages this metric to achieve more stable and effective training. By improving sampling efficiency and reasoning quality, our approach contributes to more reliable LLM post-training on complex reasoning tasks such as mathematics. While there are many potential societal implications of advancing LLM reasoning capabilities, we do not identify any that require specific emphasis here.

Difference between IB-Score and Entropy IB-Score proposed in Section 3.2 evaluates policy models overall uncertainty as well as its ability of confidence allocation. Although entropy also measures policy uncertainty, we summarize the differences between IB-Score and single policy entropy as follows: IB-Score is surrogate objective derived from the Information Bottleneck theory that can evaluate the explorationexploitation trade-off of policy model. It takes into account not only the models confidence but also the reward gains from different branches, which makes IB-Score more comprehensive metric than single policy entropy. Instead of token-level policy entropy, IB-Score focuses entropy computation at the step-level to obtain results that better reflect actual model decision-making. This makes IB-Score less susceptible to the influence of outlier tokensa particularly pronounced issue in reinforcement learning (RL), where the mismatch between of the inference and the training model often leads to precision discrepancies that can degrade inference/training performance (Ma et al., 2025; He & Lab, 2025). 13 Long Live The Balance: Information Bottleneck Driven Tree-based Policy Optimization B. More Implementation Details B.1. More Details of IB-TPO We implement IB-TPO on ms-swift (Zhao et al., 2025a), which is widely-used light-weight infrastructure for model finetuning and integrates vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023) for inference acceleration. We choose recommended parameters T=0.7, top p=0.95 and top k=20 for online sampling with max truncation length of 2K. We use learning rate 1 106, and set the KL regularization weight to 0.001. For fair comparison, we applied these settings consistently across all compared methods. During IBTree expansion, we choose hyper-parameters (B0, L, K, B) = (4, 9, 1, 1) for experiments, which means we will sample 4 initial trajectories from root node (i.e., problem q) for each tree, and branch at the non-leaf node with the highest IB-Score with one more trajectory in every expansion iteration. After tree sampling, = 12 trajectories will be collected for RL optimization. B.2. More Details of Compared Baselines We conduct experiments of GRPO baselines with clip-higher and entropy regularization in ms-swift (Zhao et al., 2025a), since both are natively supported by the training framework. We choose the most commonly used parameters for experiments. Specifically, we find that ϵh = 0.28 for clip-higher and ω = 0.003 for entropy regularization could lead to performance degradation in the final stages of training. Therefore, we carefully retune the hyper-parameters and choose ϵh = 0.26 and ω = 0.001 for their better performances, which are also adopted by recent approaches (Khatri et al., 2025; Lei et al., 2025). We select IBRO (Lei et al., 2025) as the baseline that combines Information Bottleneck (IB) with online RL. Since there is no official open-source implementation available to date and its implementation is relatively straightforward, we directly implement IBRO within our codebase by adding following code to compute IBRO loss: def _compute_loss_and_metrics(self, model, inputs): # original code # ibro_loss = compute_mean(entropy * advantage) loss = loss - ibro_coeff * ibro_loss # original code # # new line # new line and we use the officially recommended coefficient 0.005 in our experiment for comparison. Notably, the recent work IIB-LPO (Deng et al., 2026) also introduces IB into online reinforcement training. However, since its IB regularization term are highly similar with IBRO and its code has not been open-sourced yet, we did not include it in our comparison. We choose TreeRL (Hou et al., 2025) and TreePO (Li et al., 2025) as the primary tree-based online RL baselines because they have demonstrated state-of-the-art performances, and both of them have optimized tree sampling efficiency to meet the high-efficiency demands of online RL. Specifically, TreeRL propose EPTree, an entropy-guided tree search strategy that selectively branches at the the token with highest token-entropy, which has similar tree expansion pipeline to our IBTree. For fair comparison, we aligned the tree expansion hyper-parameters of EPTree and IBTree in our experiments (i.e., (B0, L, K, B) = (4, 9, 1, 1)). Moreover, TreePO uses fixed step length for generation and limits both the number of branches per tree node and the maximum tree width to avoid excessive tree sampling cost. Similarly, we align the initial divergence of TreePO (number of branches for root node) with our method (i.e., B0 = 4), which is slightly higher than that of other tree nodes. This strategy is shown to benefit model performance in experiments of TreePO because it improves models exploration diversity. And we set the step length as 256, the default number of branches as 2, the max width of tree as = 12. B.3. Prompts During RL training, we uniformly set the system prompt as follows: System Prompt Please reason step by step, and put your final answer within boxed{}. For mathematical benchmarks, we use the same system prompt to evaluate the models. For other benchmarks, we employ dataset-specific prompts designed by evaluation framework (Team, 2024). 14 Long Live The Balance: Information Bottleneck Driven Tree-based Policy Optimization C. Comparison of pass@K Accuracy Previous approach (Yue et al., 2025) indicates that pass@K can reflect models exploration-exploitation capabilities, and standard online RL suffers from lower pass@K when using large K, indicating the limited searching space of RL optimized model. To validate the effectiveness of our method, we compare the pass@K accuracy in Figure 7 complement to avg@32 accuracy. Our method achieves significant lead in pass@K. Figure 7. Comparison of pass@K accuracy trained on Qwen3-8B-Base. 15 Long Live The Balance: Information Bottleneck Driven Tree-based Policy Optimization C.3. Comparison Wall-clock Runtime IBTree allows sampling 50% more trajectories (8 12) than independent sampling strategy under the same token budget. However, multi-iteration tree expansion reduces the parallelism of trajectory generation for the same problem (also mentioned in TreeRL (Hou et al., 2025)), which potentially impacts IBTrees actual runtime, even though we can maximize the utilization of the inference engines decoding width by performing parallel expansion across multiple trees. To comprehensively evaluate the practical runtime efficiency of IBTree, we compare the GPU wall-clock runtime of IBTree and independent sampling in Figure 8. Figure 8. Comparison of wall-clock time consumption with respect to the number of trees sampled in parallel. For ease of comparison, we set the time consumption of independent sampling on single tree as baseline (1.0). As shown, benefiting from the parallel decoding technology of the modern inference engine (Kwon et al., 2023), the gap of average runtime per tree (problem) between IBTree (G = 12) and Independent Sampling (G = 8) becomes increasingly smaller as the trees parallelism increases. Despite this, in future work we will prioritize reducing the wall-clock runtime of tree sampling as one of the key directions for improvement and enhancing its practical value. Moreover, we have observed that asynchronous expansion across different trees can reduce inter-iteration waiting, yielding about 15% further runtime reduction in our pilot implementation. Based on this, we conduct an additional experiment comparing GRPO (G=8) with IBTPO (G=8) in wall-clock-aligned manner. Shown in Table 7, IBTPO has lower sampling wall-clock runtime than GRPO due to efficient tree search, while it still achieving better performance, demonstrating the superiority of our proposed framework. Case Study of IBTree We provide several examples of IBTree sampling in Figure 9, Figure 10 and Figure 11. IBTree select branching nodes based on IB-Score, which takes into account both the models confidence in different branches and the expected rewards from those branches, enabling IBTree to perform efficient and diverse exploration. Moreover, we can observe that using nn as delimiter effectively distinguishes solution steps with independent semantic meaning, which is more effective and flexible than using fixed step length (Li et al., 2025).
